title: UNDERSTANDING HTML WITH LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS

structured.summary: This paper explores fine-tuning large pretrained language models (LLMs) on raw HTML text for three canonical tasks—semantic classification, description generation, and autonomous web navigation—demonstrating that encoder-decoder LLMs, especially T5 variants, achieve strong performance and data efficiency without specialized architectures. The authors introduce a large-scale auto-labeled dataset for description generation and achieve state-of-the-art supervised results on MiniWoB navigation using significantly less data than prior work.

strengths.point: Comprehensive evaluation of multiple LLM architectures and sizes on diverse HTML understanding tasks
strengths.evidence: The paper benchmarks encoder-only, encoder-decoder, and decoder-only models (e.g., BERT, T5, LaMDA, PaLM) across semantic classification, description generation, and navigation, showing consistent superiority of encoder-decoder T5 models.

strengths.point: Demonstration of strong data efficiency and improved performance via fine-tuning pretrained LLMs
strengths.evidence: Fine-tuned models outperform those trained from scratch by up to 12.7% on classification and achieve 51.8% success on MiniWoB navigation with 192x less data than prior supervised learning baselines.

strengths.point: Introduction and open-sourcing of a large-scale, auto-labeled description generation dataset distilled from CommonCrawl
strengths.evidence: The dataset contains 85K samples generated using HTML `for` attributes, enabling training of models that generate natural language descriptions of HTML elements.

strengths.point: Insightful analysis of the impact of pretraining, model architecture, and context window size
strengths.evidence: Pretraining on natural language corpora improves performance by up to 34%, encoder-decoder architectures outperform others, and snippet extraction is necessary to handle context window limitations.

strengths.point: Achieving new state-of-the-art supervised learning results on MiniWoB navigation with significantly less data
strengths.evidence: WebN-T5-3B achieves 51.8% success, surpassing previous SL models trained on millions of demonstrations by 16%.

weaknesses.point: Limited comparison to recent domain-specific HTML/web understanding models beyond MarkupLM
weaknesses.evidence: The paper benchmarks only against MarkupLM for semantic classification, omitting other specialized models leveraging DOM trees or multimodal inputs.

weaknesses.point: Lack of ablation studies on snippet extraction parameters and their effect on model performance
weaknesses.evidence: No experiments vary snippet size, selection heuristics, or marking of salient elements, leaving unclear the robustness and sensitivity of preprocessing choices.

weaknesses.point: No evaluation on full raw HTML pages without snippet extraction for navigation
weaknesses.evidence: Navigation experiments rely exclusively on snippet extraction, raising questions about generalization to real-world long and complex HTML pages.

weaknesses.point: Insufficient analysis of error modes and qualitative examples for navigation failures
weaknesses.evidence: Despite reporting a 51.8% success rate, the paper lacks detailed error breakdowns or illustrative failure cases.

weaknesses.point: Missing details on fine-tuning hyperparameters, training protocols, and code/model release
weaknesses.evidence: The paper does not specify learning rates, batch sizes, epochs, or optimizer settings, hindering reproducibility.

weaknesses.point: No evaluation of model robustness to malformed or adversarial HTML inputs
weaknesses.evidence: Real-world HTML often contains noise or errors, but robustness under such conditions is not assessed.

weaknesses.point: Limited exploration of few-shot prompting and prompt engineering across models
weaknesses.evidence: Few-shot experiments are restricted to PaLM-540B without exploring advanced prompting strategies or other LLMs.

weaknesses.point: No discussion of inference latency, memory usage, or computational cost for large models
weaknesses.evidence: Practical deployment considerations for models up to 62B parameters are not addressed.

weaknesses.point: No investigation of multilingual or cross-lingual performance despite dataset source
weaknesses.evidence: CommonCrawl contains multilingual data, but experiments focus solely on English.

weaknesses.point: Lack of analysis on how models internally represent or leverage HTML hierarchical structure
weaknesses.evidence: Although removing closing tags reduces performance, no probing or attention analysis is provided.

questions.question: How do different snippet extraction strategies affect model performance and robustness across tasks?
questions.motivation: Understanding sensitivity to preprocessing is critical for deploying models on diverse and large-scale web pages.

questions.question: Can the models generalize effectively to full-length, complex HTML pages without snippet extraction?
questions.motivation: Real-world web automation requires handling entire pages; evaluating this is essential for practical applicability.

questions.question: How do these fine-tuned LLMs perform on multilingual or non-English HTML content?
questions.motivation: Web content is globally diverse; cross-lingual capabilities would broaden the utility of these models.

questions.question: What are the computational and latency trade-offs when deploying large LLMs for real-time web navigation?
questions.motivation: Resource constraints and responsiveness are key factors for real-world autonomous agents.

questions.question: How can model interpretability be improved to understand how HTML structure is internally represented and utilized?
questions.motivation: Insights into model reasoning can guide architecture design and error mitigation.

critique_points.point_001: Limited comparison to recent domain-specific HTML/web understanding models beyond MarkupLM. The paper benchmarks only against MarkupLM for semantic classification, omitting other specialized models leveraging DOM trees or multimodal inputs
critique_points.point_002: Lack of ablation studies on snippet extraction parameters and their effect on model performance. No experiments vary snippet size, selection heuristics, or marking of salient elements, leaving unclear the robustness and sensitivity of preprocessing choices
critique_points.point_003: No evaluation on full raw HTML pages without snippet extraction for navigation. Navigation experiments rely exclusively on snippet extraction, raising questions about generalization to real-world long and complex HTML pages
critique_points.point_004: Insufficient analysis of error modes and qualitative examples for navigation failures. Despite reporting a 51.8% success rate, the paper lacks detailed error breakdowns or illustrative failure cases
critique_points.point_005: Missing details on fine-tuning hyperparameters, training protocols, and code/model release. The paper does not specify learning rates, batch sizes, epochs, or optimizer settings, hindering reproducibility
critique_points.point_006: No evaluation of model robustness to malformed or adversarial HTML inputs. Real-world HTML often contains noise or errors, but robustness under such conditions is not assessed
critique_points.point_007: Limited exploration of few-shot prompting and prompt engineering across models. Few-shot experiments are restricted to PaLM-540B without exploring advanced prompting strategies or other LLMs
critique_points.point_008: No discussion of inference latency, memory usage, or computational cost for large models. Practical deployment considerations for models up to 62B parameters are not addressed
critique_points.point_009: No investigation of multilingual or cross-lingual performance despite dataset source. CommonCrawl contains multilingual data, but experiments focus solely on English
critique_points.point_010: Lack of analysis on how models internally represent or leverage HTML hierarchical structure. Although removing closing tags reduces performance, no probing or attention analysis is provided
